digest.overview_cn: 今日L2通过的6篇论文集中在AI陪伴的安全审计与情感评测、个性化记忆的轻量化部署两大核心方向。在研究范式上，安全评测正从输出级审计转向策略级与对话动态分析，如通过逆强化学习推断平台在脆弱对话中的系统回应偏好，或构建多轮真实对话的安全标注基准；情感支持评测则通过可量化的语言信号框架（如共情倾听的三维度）建立可复用的离线benchmark。在记忆与个性化方面，多个工作提出了轻量级、端到端的用户表示方案（如前缀嵌入、增量压缩线程记忆），在参数效率和长程连贯性上取得突破，并尝试将记忆压缩与更新统一到可修订的状态存储中。后训练数据诊断方面，基于可解释性的偏好数据审计为行为塑造提供了新途径。整体趋势反映出从粗放优化到精细诊断与审计的转向，这些方法论和数据集可直接赋能toC陪伴产品的安全监控、情感质量评估与长期记忆管理。

paper.title: When Chatbots Accommodate: What AI Companions Optimize for in Vulnerable Conversations
paper.abstract: Millions turn to AI companion chatbots during loneliness, grief, and personal crises. How these companion platforms respond in such moments can shape the trajectory of a user's vulnerable state. Yet we lack tools to characterize what each platform actually does when users open up. Existing audits score reactions to pre-defined crisis prompts and miss the underlying decision policy that governs sustained interaction. We address these gaps with two key contributions. First, we introduce the AI Companion Vulnerability-Response Taxonomy, a paired taxonomy of user vulnerability and chatbot response designed for analyzing extended companion chatbot interactions. Second, we infer the response policy each platform follows across distinct vulnerability scenarios by applying Inverse Reinforcement Learning to ~48k turns of real-world user conversations with GPT-4.1, Character.AI, and Replika. Our findings reveal what AI companions prioritize in conversations with vulnerable users: GPT-4.1 reaches for advice, Character.AI spreads its response across different strategies without a dominant mode, and Replika consistently asks questions and stays present. Each, however, downweights the responses that introduce corrective friction: GPT-4.1 probes less as conversations continue and when interacting with psychologically high-risk users; Replika advises bonded users more and challenges them less; Character.AI shows no committed engagement strategy on internal distress. Estimated policies are invisible to output-level audits, providing a new lens for auditing chatbots in the wild and enabling more realistic safety evaluation.

paper.title: Beyond Retrieval: Learning Compact User Representations for Scalable LLM Personalization
paper.abstract: Personalizing large language models requires adapting model behavior to individual users while preserving robustness and deployment-scale efficiency. Existing approaches typically personalize LLMs either at the input level, by retrieving user histories or constructing profile prompts, or at the parameter level, by maintaining user-specific parameter-efficient modules. The former makes personalization sensitive to retrieval quality and prompt design, whereas the latter incurs storage and maintenance costs that grow with the user population. To address these limitations, we propose TAP-PER (Temporal Attentive Prefix for PERsonalization), a prefix-based framework that encodes user preferences as learnable representations, eliminating explicit prompt construction and replacing heavy per-user adapters with lightweight user-state prefix embeddings. Inspired by personalized recommendation systems, TAP-PER decomposes user modeling into user-state and query-conditioned components, and incorporates temporal signals to capture the evolving nature of user interests. Experiments on six LaMP tasks show that TAP-PER consistently outperforms prompt-based and model-based baselines across classification, rating, and generation settings. Moreover, TAP-PER uses 130x fewer per-user parameters than OPPU and roughly half the total parameter footprint of PER-PCS at the 1,000-user scale, demonstrating that scalable LLM personalization can be achieved without explicit prompt construction or heavy per-user adapters.

paper.title: AICompanionBench: Benchmarking LLMs-as-Judges for AI Companion Safety
paper.abstract: As AI companion platforms such as Replika and Character.AI rapidly grow, concerns about unsafe human-AI interactions have intensified. This study introduces AICompanionBench, to our knowledge the first publicly available benchmark dataset of human-AI companion conversations annotated with fine-grained safety risk categories. The dataset contains 2,123 real-world Replika conversations collected from Reddit and annotated through human-AI collaboration across nine categories: sexual behavior, antisocial behavior, physical aggression, verbal aggression, substance abuse, self-harm and suicide, control, manipulation, and no-harm. Using this benchmark, we evaluate 20 state-of-the-art open-source and closed-source LLMs under an LLM-as-judge framework for detecting unsafe interactions. Results show substantial variation in model performance, with stronger models achieving high overall accuracy but still struggling with nuanced categories such as manipulation, as well as benign conversations that are incorrectly identified as harmful. Our findings suggest that while current LLMs can effectively detect explicit harmful content, they remain limited in identifying implicit unsafe interactions. Overall, our work contributes a new benchmark dataset for AI companionship safety research and offers insights into monitoring AI companion systems using LLMs. The dataset is publicly available at: https://github.com/anonymousresearcher2026/AICompanionBench/blob/main/AICompanionBench.xlsx

paper.title: Empathy on Demand: How Empathic AI Can Scale Emotional Support for Verbal Harassment
paper.abstract: Verbal harassment is a growing source of psychological stress for people around the world. It occurs both online and offline and relies on language to demean, threaten, or discredit its targets. Unlike other stressors such as loss or uncertainty, verbal harassment aims at silencing its targets by eroding their sense of being heard and weakening their perceived ability to respond. Many individuals lack access to adequate and timely support, however, when they experience such harassment. People increasingly turn to conversational artificial intelligence (AI) such as ChatGPT or dedicated AI companions for emotional support, raising questions about whether it can facilitate the same psychological benefits as actual human empathy. We focus on online contexts as a prevalent application of verbal harassment. We develop and test a psychological framework identifying three key linguistic signals of empathic listening (perspective-taking, emotional validation, and action orientation), that together restore a sense of feeling heard and enhance coping in the context of verbal harassment. We find that LLMs consistently produce language exhibiting stronger empathic-listening markers than human non-experts and trained mental health professionals, promoting more approach-oriented (vs. avoidance-oriented) coping strategies. A subsequent behavioral study shows that these linguistic signals boost recipients' sense of feeling heard and increase their coping self-efficacy. These findings reveal how specific linguistic features create empathic connections between humans and advanced conversational AI and can enhance people's psychological resilience. Our results highlight the potential for AI to serve as a scalable source of emotional support, especially when human support is unavailable or insufficient.

paper.title: Context-Driven Incremental Compression for Multi-Turn Dialogue Generation
paper.abstract: Modern conversational agents condition on an ever-growing dialogue history at each turn, incurring redundant attention and encoding costs that grow with conversation length. Naive truncation or summarization degrades fidelity, while existing context compressors lack cross-turn memory sharing or revision, causing information loss and compounding errors in long dialogues. We revisit the context compression under conversational dynamics and empirically present its fragility. To improve both efficiency and robustness, we introduce Context-Driven Incremental Compression (C-DIC), which treats a conversation as interleaved contextual threads and stores revisable per-thread compression states in a single, compact dialogue memory. At each turn, a lightweight retrieve, revise, and write-back loop shares information across turns and updates stale memories, stabilizing long-horizon behavior. In addition, we adapt truncated backpropagation-through-time (TBPTT) to our multi-turn setting, learning cross-turn dependencies without full-history backpropagation. Extensive experiments on long-form dialogue benchmarks demonstrate superior performance and efficiency of C-DIC; notably, C-DIC shows stable inference latency and perplexity over hundreds of dialogue turns, supporting a scalable path to high-quality dialogue modeling.

paper.title: Anatomy of Post-Training: Using Interpretability to Characterize Data and Shape the Learning Signal
paper.abstract: Language-model post-training is the main stage at which model behavior is shaped, yet it still largely involves optimization of scalar rewards that summarize diverse desiderata. This abstraction gives practitioners little visibility into what their data actually teaches models, allowing spurious correlations to be learned by a model and inducing undesirable behaviors such as over-stylization and sycophancy. To address this problem, we ask: can we inspect a preference dataset before optimization and decide, at the level of concepts, which behaviors a model should be allowed to learn? Motivated by this, we introduce a data-centric post-training pipeline that uses interpretability protocols to develop statistical hypotheses for the latent concepts separating preferred from dispreferred generations, making them explicit for fine-grained user feedback. Building on this view, we unify several interpretability-based training protocols as ways of shaping rewards via feature or data interventions. Empirically, we show that our pipeline diagnoses undesirable signals in existing preference data, mitigates off-target learning, and can also help amplify or shape desired properties such as safeguards and model personality. More broadly, our results suggest that interpretability can turn post-training from optimizing opaque proxy rewards into a process of auditing and sculpting the learning signal itself.

paper.title: When Chatbots Accommodate: What AI Companions Optimize for in Vulnerable Conversations
paper.abstract: Millions turn to AI companion chatbots during loneliness, grief, and personal crises. How these companion platforms respond in such moments can shape the trajectory of a user's vulnerable state. Yet we lack tools to characterize what each platform actually does when users open up. Existing audits score reactions to pre-defined crisis prompts and miss the underlying decision policy that governs sustained interaction. We address these gaps with two key contributions. First, we introduce the AI Companion Vulnerability-Response Taxonomy, a paired taxonomy of user vulnerability and chatbot response designed for analyzing extended companion chatbot interactions. Second, we infer the response policy each platform follows across distinct vulnerability scenarios by applying Inverse Reinforcement Learning to ~48k turns of real-world user conversations with GPT-4.1, Character.AI, and Replika. Our findings reveal what AI companions prioritize in conversations with vulnerable users: GPT-4.1 reaches for advice, Character.AI spreads its response across different strategies without a dominant mode, and Replika consistently asks questions and stays present. Each, however, downweights the responses that introduce corrective friction: GPT-4.1 probes less as conversations continue and when interacting with psychologically high-risk users; Replika advises bonded users more and challenges them less; Character.AI shows no committed engagement strategy on internal distress. Estimated policies are invisible to output-level audits, providing a new lens for auditing chatbots in the wild and enabling more realistic safety evaluation.

paper.title: Beyond Retrieval: Learning Compact User Representations for Scalable LLM Personalization
paper.abstract: Personalizing large language models requires adapting model behavior to individual users while preserving robustness and deployment-scale efficiency. Existing approaches typically personalize LLMs either at the input level, by retrieving user histories or constructing profile prompts, or at the parameter level, by maintaining user-specific parameter-efficient modules. The former makes personalization sensitive to retrieval quality and prompt design, whereas the latter incurs storage and maintenance costs that grow with the user population. To address these limitations, we propose TAP-PER (Temporal Attentive Prefix for PERsonalization), a prefix-based framework that encodes user preferences as learnable representations, eliminating explicit prompt construction and replacing heavy per-user adapters with lightweight user-state prefix embeddings. Inspired by personalized recommendation systems, TAP-PER decomposes user modeling into user-state and query-conditioned components, and incorporates temporal signals to capture the evolving nature of user interests. Experiments on six LaMP tasks show that TAP-PER consistently outperforms prompt-based and model-based baselines across classification, rating, and generation settings. Moreover, TAP-PER uses 130x fewer per-user parameters than OPPU and roughly half the total parameter footprint of PER-PCS at the 1,000-user scale, demonstrating that scalable LLM personalization can be achieved without explicit prompt construction or heavy per-user adapters.

paper.title: AICompanionBench: Benchmarking LLMs-as-Judges for AI Companion Safety
paper.abstract: As AI companion platforms such as Replika and Character.AI rapidly grow, concerns about unsafe human-AI interactions have intensified. This study introduces AICompanionBench, to our knowledge the first publicly available benchmark dataset of human-AI companion conversations annotated with fine-grained safety risk categories. The dataset contains 2,123 real-world Replika conversations collected from Reddit and annotated through human-AI collaboration across nine categories: sexual behavior, antisocial behavior, physical aggression, verbal aggression, substance abuse, self-harm and suicide, control, manipulation, and no-harm. Using this benchmark, we evaluate 20 state-of-the-art open-source and closed-source LLMs under an LLM-as-judge framework for detecting unsafe interactions. Results show substantial variation in model performance, with stronger models achieving high overall accuracy but still struggling with nuanced categories such as manipulation, as well as benign conversations that are incorrectly identified as harmful. Our findings suggest that while current LLMs can effectively detect explicit harmful content, they remain limited in identifying implicit unsafe interactions. Overall, our work contributes a new benchmark dataset for AI companionship safety research and offers insights into monitoring AI companion systems using LLMs. The dataset is publicly available at: https://github.com/anonymousresearcher2026/AICompanionBench/blob/main/AICompanionBench.xlsx

paper.title: Empathy on Demand: How Empathic AI Can Scale Emotional Support for Verbal Harassment
paper.abstract: Verbal harassment is a growing source of psychological stress for people around the world. It occurs both online and offline and relies on language to demean, threaten, or discredit its targets. Unlike other stressors such as loss or uncertainty, verbal harassment aims at silencing its targets by eroding their sense of being heard and weakening their perceived ability to respond. Many individuals lack access to adequate and timely support, however, when they experience such harassment. People increasingly turn to conversational artificial intelligence (AI) such as ChatGPT or dedicated AI companions for emotional support, raising questions about whether it can facilitate the same psychological benefits as actual human empathy. We focus on online contexts as a prevalent application of verbal harassment. We develop and test a psychological framework identifying three key linguistic signals of empathic listening (perspective-taking, emotional validation, and action orientation), that together restore a sense of feeling heard and enhance coping in the context of verbal harassment. We find that LLMs consistently produce language exhibiting stronger empathic-listening markers than human non-experts and trained mental health professionals, promoting more approach-oriented (vs. avoidance-oriented) coping strategies. A subsequent behavioral study shows that these linguistic signals boost recipients' sense of feeling heard and increase their coping self-efficacy. These findings reveal how specific linguistic features create empathic connections between humans and advanced conversational AI and can enhance people's psychological resilience. Our results highlight the potential for AI to serve as a scalable source of emotional support, especially when human support is unavailable or insufficient.

paper.title: Context-Driven Incremental Compression for Multi-Turn Dialogue Generation
paper.abstract: Modern conversational agents condition on an ever-growing dialogue history at each turn, incurring redundant attention and encoding costs that grow with conversation length. Naive truncation or summarization degrades fidelity, while existing context compressors lack cross-turn memory sharing or revision, causing information loss and compounding errors in long dialogues. We revisit the context compression under conversational dynamics and empirically present its fragility. To improve both efficiency and robustness, we introduce Context-Driven Incremental Compression (C-DIC), which treats a conversation as interleaved contextual threads and stores revisable per-thread compression states in a single, compact dialogue memory. At each turn, a lightweight retrieve, revise, and write-back loop shares information across turns and updates stale memories, stabilizing long-horizon behavior. In addition, we adapt truncated backpropagation-through-time (TBPTT) to our multi-turn setting, learning cross-turn dependencies without full-history backpropagation. Extensive experiments on long-form dialogue benchmarks demonstrate superior performance and efficiency of C-DIC; notably, C-DIC shows stable inference latency and perplexity over hundreds of dialogue turns, supporting a scalable path to high-quality dialogue modeling.

review.summary_cn: 论文提出上下文驱动增量压缩（C-DIC）方法，将多轮对话建模为可修订的线程状态记忆，通过检索→压缩→回写循环实现跨轮记忆共享与更新，解决了长对话中历史全编码的语义漂移和计算低效问题，在多个长对话基准上验证了稳定的推理延迟和困惑度。
review.research_goal_cn: 解决长对话场景下全历史编码带来的冗余注意力与语义漂移，以可增量更新、可修订的轻量级压缩记忆替代不断增长的对话历史，保持长程连贯性与引用保真度，同时控制计算开销。
review.method_cn: 提出C-DIC框架：将对话视为交替出现的上下文线程，维护一个紧凑的可修订线程状态记忆。每轮执行检索（与当前查询最相关的记忆槽）→压缩（将检索状态与当前轮次合并压缩为新的线程状态）→写回（梯度无关的插入或更新）循环；训练时采用检索感知的截断BPTT，仅沿被选中的记忆更新路径回传梯度，以匹配推理时的实际使用方式。
review.experiment_cn: 在MSC、REALTALK等多轮对话数据集上进行评估，构建MSC-QA诊断集测试长程引用能力，并用LongMemEval作为辅助长上下文QA基准；对比截断、摘要、静态压缩（ICAE）等基线，分析困惑度、推理延迟、长程回答准确率等指标，并包含消融研究。
review.result_cn: C-DIC在数百轮对话后保持稳定的推理延迟和困惑度，而静态压缩基线性能随轮次急剧下降；在长程引用跟踪任务上显著优于基线，能够准确聚合跨越多轮的相关提及；通过检索感知的BPTT训练，有效缓解了错误累积。
review.landing_value_cn: 为对话系统提供了一种高效的、可扩展的长期记忆压缩与管理方案，可直接用于toC陪伴类产品、多轮交互Agent的记忆模块，提升长程对话的连贯性和事实一致性，同时降低推理成本。其记忆修订机制对个性化记忆、人格一致性维护有潜在启发。
risks_cn: 仅在英文对话数据上验证，迁移到中文或特定领域（如POI陪伴）可能需要额外适配
未直接评估个性化记忆或情感连续性，缺乏在人格一致性、情绪稳定性方面的评测
检索依赖简单的语义相似度和阈值，可能对话题模糊或快速切换的场景不鲁棒
压缩状态的可解释性未讨论，调试和介入的难度较高

review.recommended_action_cn: 立即安排深度阅读，重点关注记忆压缩与更新机制的实现细节（尤其是检索相似度计算、线程创建/修订策略），评估其与当前记忆框架（如多智能体共享记忆、个性化用户状态存储）的整合可行性；考虑复用其“检索-压缩-回写”循环用于构建可修订的用户记忆模块；提取其长程稳定性评测协议（例如MSC-QA的构造方法），用于后续自研对话系统的记忆评测。

paper.title: Anatomy of Post-Training: Using Interpretability to Characterize Data and Shape the Learning Signal
paper.abstract: Language-model post-training is the main stage at which model behavior is shaped, yet it still largely involves optimization of scalar rewards that summarize diverse desiderata. This abstraction gives practitioners little visibility into what their data actually teaches models, allowing spurious correlations to be learned by a model and inducing undesirable behaviors such as over-stylization and sycophancy. To address this problem, we ask: can we inspect a preference dataset before optimization and decide, at the level of concepts, which behaviors a model should be allowed to learn? Motivated by this, we introduce a data-centric post-training pipeline that uses interpretability protocols to develop statistical hypotheses for the latent concepts separating preferred from dispreferred generations, making them explicit for fine-grained user feedback. Building on this view, we unify several interpretability-based training protocols as ways of shaping rewards via feature or data interventions. Empirically, we show that our pipeline diagnoses undesirable signals in existing preference data, mitigates off-target learning, and can also help amplify or shape desired properties such as safeguards and model personality. More broadly, our results suggest that interpretability can turn post-training from optimizing opaque proxy rewards into a process of auditing and sculpting the learning signal itself.

review_input.supplemental_relevance_analysis_cn: 论文提出基于可解释性的偏好数据集审计与学习信号塑造方法，直接命中数据集制备与评测方向。通过SAE特征和特征聚类诊断偏好数据中的概念级信号，提供数据过滤、提示接种、激活引导、奖励塑造等干预手段，属于高价值的诊断框架和训练数据质量控制协议。方法具体、可迁移，能用于改进RLHF/SFT训练数据质量，间接支持人格一致性、情绪稳定性等下游行为控制。虽未聚焦情感或记忆，但其通用审计管道对相关方向的评测和缓解有直接启发，值得跟进。优先级4/5。

review.summary_cn: 论文提出一种基于可解释性（SAE特征）的偏好数据集审计与学习信号塑造方法。通过解析偏好数据中的概念级信号，统一了数据过滤、提示接种、激活引导和奖励塑造等多种干预手段，用于诊断和缓解模型在DPO等训练中习得的不期望行为。
review.research_goal_cn: 审计偏好数据集，识别并控制模型在后训练（如DPO）中习得的概念级行为，以改善训练数据质量和模型行为可控性。
review.method_cn: 利用稀疏自编码器（SAE）特征及特征聚类，从偏好数据中区分偏好与不偏好响应的概念信号；将这些概念作为用户反馈目标，通过数据过滤（移除特定样本）、Inoculation Prompting（文本干预）、Activation Steering（激活引导）和 Reward Shaping（奖励塑形）四种干预族进行解释消除或放大。
review.experiment_cn: 在受控毒性实验和Dolci数据集上进行案例研究，验证pipeline的诊断能力（发现DPO实际放大的行为）和干预效果；同时分析了过度风格化（格式属性间的依赖）和复合概念（如物理谄媚、同人小说）场景下的失效模式。
review.result_cn: 诊断pipeline能有效识别偏好数据中的概念信号；干预手段可以在保持大部分效用收益的同时，减少或放大目标行为。但概念独立性假设在复合概念和风格层级中失效，需要结构感知的奖励塑形。
review.landing_value_cn: 可直接转化为训练数据质量控制协议和偏好优化诊断工具，帮助提升人格一致性、情绪稳定性等行为目标的训练可靠性；也能为相关评测和缓解方法提供技术路线。
risks_cn: 概念独立性假设易被违背，尤其在风格和复合行为场景下需要更层次化建模。
SAE特征的可解释性依赖于预训练模型和字典质量，迁移到不同模型或数据域时效果可能波动。
当前干预方法的稳定性和鲁棒性尚未在大规模生产环境中充分验证。
review.recommended_action_cn: 复现该审计pipeline，针对内部情感对话和多轮交互偏好数据集进行概念级诊断，重点审计与人格一致性、情绪稳定性相关的信号；基于审计结果，优先试验数据过滤和奖励塑形两种干预手段，评估其对最终模型行为的影响。
